content: Improving global health aid effectiveness requires: (1) Country ownership - aligning with national health strategies rather than donor priorities; (2) Harmonization - donors coordinating to reduce transaction costs (Sector Wide Approaches - SWAps); (3) Use of country systems - channeling funds through government budgets when governance adequate; (4) Focus on results - measurable outcomes over inputs; (5) Mutual accountability - transparent reporting by both donors and recipients. Paris Declaration (2005) and Accra Agenda (2008) established these principles, but implementation remains limited (<30% of aid uses country systems). Successful examples: Rwanda's health system strengthening through pooled donor funds achieving >90% immunization coverage and 60% reduction in child mortality 2000-2015; Thailand's universal coverage achieved with minimal external aid through domestic financing.
